front: Novel: an unnamed biracial narrator decides to pass as white after witnessing a lynching; ends wondering if he 'traded his birthright for a mess of pottage'
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator first learns he is biracial when his principal asks 'all of the white scholars' in his class to stand
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator plays ragtime piano for a rich white gentleman and travels to Europe before deciding to pass as white
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Poem: God says 'I'm lonely -- I'll make me a world'; part of 'Seven Negro Sermons in Verse' illustrated by Aaron Douglas
back: The Creation in God's Trombones (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: a funeral sermon in which Sister Caroline is taken to heaven from her 'big bed of pain'
back: Go Down, Death in God's Trombones (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: warns 'your arm's too short to box with God' about a young man seduced by the pleasures of Babylon
back: The Prodigal Son in God's Trombones (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: known as the 'Black National Anthem'; Maya Angelou's class sang it at graduation in I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
back: Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Novel: while in Paris, the narrator sees his father and half-sister at a performance of Gounod's Faust
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator's classmate 'Shiny' becomes an orator; encountering Shiny in New York inspires the narrator to tell his fiancee a secret
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator drops out of Atlanta University, hides in a Pullman car closet to reach Jacksonville, and works as a reader in a cigar factory
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator plays ragtime renditions of Mendelssohn's Wedding March while touring Europe with a benefactor
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator's absentee father is described as having 'the best blood of the South'
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Novel: the narrator had youthful feelings for a violin-playing character called 'she of the brown eyes'
back: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man (James Weldon Johnson)
work: The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man

front: Poem: drew from the spiritual 'Go Down, Moses' for a poem described as a 'funeral sermon' in a 1927 collection
back: Go Down, Death in God's Trombones (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: the speaker asks 'At the creation, ever heard a theme / Nobler than Go Down, Moses?'
back: O Black and Unknown Bards (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: Augusta Savage created a harp-shaped sculpture named for this song, showing a boy kneeling before people in descending height
back: Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: describes the bitterness of the 'chastening rod' and urges listeners to sing 'till earth and heaven ring'
back: Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Poem: a saxophone cover of this song precedes the opening credits of Do the Right Thing
back: Lift Ev'ry Voice and Sing (James Weldon Johnson)

front: Author: used the phrase 'Jes Grew' to describe the earliest ragtime songs in The Book of American Negro Poetry; Ishmael Reed borrowed this for Mumbo Jumbo
back: James Weldon Johnson

front: Author: declared Gertrude Stein the first white writer to write a love story about Black people as 'normal members of the human family' (re: 'Melanctha')
back: James Weldon Johnson
